## Scope and Contents

From the Series:

See more See less

Series I consists of the correspondence of Neumann family members, but primarily that of Friedrich (almost always called Fritz) and Edith Neumann. Many are from friends in England while they lived in Palestine and from friends in Palestine after they left for the United States. Several folders hold letters from other family members, and one folder contains letters between the couple while they were separated in 1934-1935 while Fritz was in Zagreb.  
  
The bulk of the correspondence of this series comes from friends to the couple, as well as many from acquaintances reporting news or seeking advice. Many letters to him are religious in tone and quote passages from the Bible. Some folders hold letters that provide details on life in Palestine, especially in Haifa in the early 1950s , including the growth and conditions of the communities and the attitudes of people there. Letters from other pastors to Fritz Neumann discuss their own difficulties or problems in their congregations or seek his professional advice. However, the bulk of the letters impart news of the correspondents' lives as well as news of friends known by the Neumanns.  
  
Particularly noteworthy in this series is the folder of handwritten correspondence between Fritz Neumann and his former professor, the famous philosopher Martin Heidegger. This folder includes three lengthy letters from 1920-1921 in which he comments upon Fritz Neumann's work. The folder of his letters also contains several drafts of letters to him by Fritz Neumann.  
  
Letters to Fritz and Edith Neumann from Fritz Neumann's parents Antonie and Jacob and his brother Hans are also present in this series. Many of these are from Antonie. Such letters discuss family news, activities and health as well as provide advice to their son. The three concluding folders of this series contain correspondence much older than that of the rest of the series. Handwritten and difficult to decipher, these letters and postcards were sent to Nathan and Philipp Neumann as well as two unidentified individuals, addressed only as "Brother" (Bruder) and "Uncle" (Onkel).
